title: End of module: Cyclostationarity: Core Theory

questions.text: A cyclostationary process is one whose statistics (e.g. R_x(t,τ)) are:
choices: Periodic in t, rather than constant
Always exactly zero for all t
Independent of τ but dependent on t
questions.explanation: Cyclostationarity generalizes wide-sense stationarity: instead of R_x(t,τ) being constant in t (WSS), it's periodic in t with some period T_0 — the formal signature of an underlying clocked/periodic mechanism.

questions.text: Why are digitally modulated signals (symbol clocking, carrier, framing) cyclostationary?
choices: The periodic clocking of symbols makes the signal's statistics, not just the raw waveform, repeat periodically in time
All modulated signals happen to be strictly periodic waveforms
Modulation always removes any correlation between samples
questions.explanation: Clocking symbols at a fixed rate imposes periodicity on the signal's second-order statistics R_x(t,τ), even for a signal whose raw waveform (carrying random bits) never repeats exactly.

questions.text: The cyclic autocorrelation function R_x^α(τ) is obtained from R_x(t,τ) by:
choices: Taking the Fourier series coefficients of R_x(t,τ) as a function of t
Averaging R_x(t,τ) over τ instead of t
Taking the derivative of R_x(t,τ) with respect to t
questions.explanation: Because R_x(t,τ) is periodic in t, it has a Fourier series in t for each fixed τ; the coefficients of that series, indexed by cycle frequency α, are exactly the cyclic autocorrelation function R_x^α(τ).

questions.text: The α = 0 cyclic autocorrelation term R_x^0(τ) equals:
choices: The ordinary (non-cyclic) time-averaged autocorrelation function
Zero, always
The process's mean value
questions.explanation: Setting α = 0 in the Fourier-series formula makes the complex exponential equal to 1, so R_x^0(τ) is just the plain time-average of R_x(t,τ) — the same autocorrelation function used for WSS processes.

questions.text: The spectral correlation function (SCF) S_x^α(f) is defined as:
choices: The Fourier transform, over τ, of the cyclic autocorrelation function R_x^α(τ)
The Fourier transform, over t, of the ordinary PSD
The inverse Fourier transform of the process's mean function
questions.explanation: The SCF generalizes Wiener–Khinchin to every cycle frequency: it's the Fourier transform over the lag variable τ of R_x^α(τ), producing a two-dimensional function of ordinary frequency f and cycle frequency α.

questions.text: Why can a modulated signal's ordinary PSD look exactly like filtered noise while its SCF reveals clear structure?
choices: The PSD is only the α = 0 slice of the SCF — all the symbol-rate structure can sit at α ≠ 0, which the PSD cannot see at all
PSD and SCF are computed from unrelated data, so they can disagree for any reason
The PSD always overestimates the true signal power compared to the SCF
questions.explanation: Since the PSD is structurally restricted to the α = 0 slice of the full SCF surface, cyclostationary structure living at nonzero cycle frequencies (like the symbol rate) is completely invisible to it — this is exactly Gardner's spectral redundancy insight.

questions.text: A recording's cyclic statistics repeat with period T_0 = 0.5 ms. What is the fundamental cycle frequency α = 1/T_0, in Hz?
A signal's symbol rate is 8000 symbols/second. What is the fundamental cycle frequency, in Hz, that its cyclic autocorrelation function should show a nonzero coefficient at?
questions.explanation: The fundamental cycle frequency equals the symbol rate itself (1/T_0 where T_0 is the symbol period), so a symbol rate of 8000 symbols/second gives α = 8000 Hz (plus its integer-multiple harmonics).

questions.text: One phrase (two words): Gardner's term for the phenomenon where a cyclostationary signal has correlated spectral components spaced α apart, which noise generically lacks, is spectral ___.
questions.explanation: Gardner's "spectral redundancy" describes exactly this correlation between spectral components separated by the cycle frequency — the property a cyclostationary detector exploits and that unstructured noise does not exhibit.

questions.text: Cumulative review: back in spectral-estimation, why couldn't a single raw periodogram of a long recording reliably reveal fine spectral structure like this?
choices: Its variance at a fixed frequency doesn't shrink with recording length, so fine structure is drowned in noisy fluctuation unless variance is reduced (e.g. Welch averaging)
The periodogram cannot be computed for cyclostationary signals at all
Periodograms only work for signals with a single frequency component
questions.explanation: From spectral-estimation: the raw periodogram is an inconsistent estimator, so any real spectral structure — cyclostationary or not — needs variance-reduction techniques like Welch's method before it can be trusted, on top of the α-dimension work this module adds.
